title: Mixed-methods evaluation of school-based deworming programmes on stunting prevalence in Senegal
abstract: Background: Stunting prevalence remains a persistent challenge in Senegal, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a mixed-methods evaluation across multiple districts, enrolling 6,931 participants to evaluate the effect of school-based deworming programmes. Participants were followed for 6 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with a modest but non-significant reduction in stunting prevalence (adjusted effect size 0.39, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: School-based deworming programmes may be a scalable approach to improving stunting prevalence, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Mixed-methods evaluation of community health worker home visits on maternal mortality in Nigeria
abstract: Background: Maternal mortality remains a persistent challenge in Nigeria, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a mixed-methods evaluation across multiple districts, enrolling 6,188 participants to evaluate the effect of community health worker home visits. Participants were followed for 23 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with a large reduction in maternal mortality (adjusted effect size 0.46, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: Community health worker home visits may be a scalable approach to improving maternal mortality, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Cluster-randomised controlled trial of peer-led health education sessions on vaccine wastage rates in Rwanda
abstract: Background: Vaccine wastage rates remains a persistent challenge in Rwanda, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a cluster-randomised controlled trial across multiple districts, enrolling 8,963 participants to evaluate the effect of peer-led health education sessions. Participants were followed for 8 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with a modest but non-significant reduction in vaccine wastage rates (adjusted effect size 0.26, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: Peer-led health education sessions may be a scalable approach to improving vaccine wastage rates, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Stepped-wedge trial of task-shifted mental health counselling on childhood immunisation coverage in Uganda
abstract: Background: Childhood immunisation coverage remains a persistent challenge in Uganda, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a stepped-wedge trial across multiple districts, enrolling 9,374 participants to evaluate the effect of task-shifted mental health counselling. Participants were followed for 27 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with a large reduction in childhood immunisation coverage (adjusted effect size 0.56, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: Task-shifted mental health counselling may be a scalable approach to improving childhood immunisation coverage, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Interrupted time-series analysis of conditional cash transfers on stunting prevalence in India
abstract: Background: Stunting prevalence remains a persistent challenge in India, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a interrupted time-series analysis across multiple districts, enrolling 9,514 participants to evaluate the effect of conditional cash transfers. Participants were followed for 28 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with a modest but non-significant reduction in stunting prevalence (adjusted effect size 0.64, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: Conditional cash transfers may be a scalable approach to improving stunting prevalence, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Quasi-experimental difference-in-differences study of mobile antenatal care clinics on antenatal attendance in Kenya
abstract: Background: Antenatal attendance remains a persistent challenge in Kenya, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a quasi-experimental difference-in-differences study across multiple districts, enrolling 5,560 participants to evaluate the effect of mobile antenatal care clinics. Participants were followed for 35 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with no measurable change in antenatal attendance (adjusted effect size 0.11, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: Mobile antenatal care clinics may be a scalable approach to improving antenatal attendance, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Prospective cohort study of school-based deworming programmes on maternal mortality in Malawi
abstract: Background: Maternal mortality remains a persistent challenge in Malawi, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a prospective cohort study across multiple districts, enrolling 6,655 participants to evaluate the effect of school-based deworming programmes. Participants were followed for 7 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with no measurable change in maternal mortality (adjusted effect size 0.50, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: School-based deworming programmes may be a scalable approach to improving maternal mortality, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Mixed-methods evaluation of task-shifted mental health counselling on postpartum depression scores in Ghana
abstract: Background: Postpartum depression scores remains a persistent challenge in Ghana, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a mixed-methods evaluation across multiple districts, enrolling 9,817 participants to evaluate the effect of task-shifted mental health counselling. Participants were followed for 10 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with a modest but non-significant reduction in postpartum depression scores (adjusted effect size 0.58, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: Task-shifted mental health counselling may be a scalable approach to improving postpartum depression scores, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Pragmatic randomised trial of task-shifted mental health counselling on care-seeking behaviour in Bangladesh
abstract: Background: Care-seeking behaviour remains a persistent challenge in Bangladesh, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a pragmatic randomised trial across multiple districts, enrolling 2,251 participants to evaluate the effect of task-shifted mental health counselling. Participants were followed for 26 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with mixed effects on care-seeking behaviour (adjusted effect size 0.16, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: Task-shifted mental health counselling may be a scalable approach to improving care-seeking behaviour, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Quasi-experimental difference-in-differences study of conditional cash transfers on malaria incidence in Sierra Leone
abstract: Background: Malaria incidence remains a persistent challenge in Sierra Leone, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a quasi-experimental difference-in-differences study across multiple districts, enrolling 6,789 participants to evaluate the effect of conditional cash transfers. Participants were followed for 18 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with no measurable change in malaria incidence (adjusted effect size 0.22, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: Conditional cash transfers may be a scalable approach to improving malaria incidence, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Quasi-experimental difference-in-differences study of solar-powered cold chain storage on vaccine wastage rates in Malawi
abstract: Background: Vaccine wastage rates remains a persistent challenge in Malawi, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a quasi-experimental difference-in-differences study across multiple districts, enrolling 5,247 participants to evaluate the effect of solar-powered cold chain storage. Participants were followed for 10 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with a modest but non-significant reduction in vaccine wastage rates (adjusted effect size 0.61, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: Solar-powered cold chain storage may be a scalable approach to improving vaccine wastage rates, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Matched case-control study of mobile antenatal care clinics on out-of-pocket health expenditure in Ethiopia
abstract: Background: Out-of-pocket health expenditure remains a persistent challenge in Ethiopia, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a matched case-control study across multiple districts, enrolling 6,119 participants to evaluate the effect of mobile antenatal care clinics. Participants were followed for 12 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with a large reduction in out-of-pocket health expenditure (adjusted effect size 0.14, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: Mobile antenatal care clinics may be a scalable approach to improving out-of-pocket health expenditure, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)

title: Interrupted time-series analysis of point-of-care rapid diagnostic testing on care-seeking behaviour in Ghana
abstract: Background: Care-seeking behaviour remains a persistent challenge in Ghana, particularly in rural and peri-urban districts with limited access to services. Methods: We conducted a interrupted time-series analysis across multiple districts, enrolling 3,992 participants to evaluate the effect of point-of-care rapid diagnostic testing. Participants were followed for 29 months, with the primary outcome measured through routine facility records and household surveys. Results: The intervention was associated with a large reduction in care-seeking behaviour (adjusted effect size 0.68, 95% CI reported in full text). Secondary analyses suggested effects were strongest among the lowest wealth quintile. Conclusion: Point-of-care rapid diagnostic testing may be a scalable approach to improving care-seeking behaviour, though implementation cost and health-system readiness warrant further study. (This is synthetic mock data generated for a take-home assessment.)
